name: Chuck Liddell
winProfile: Big signature wins, lighter LHW volume

qualityWinLedger.context: Won the UFC light heavyweight title from an elite champion and defining rival.
Immediate trilogy win over an elite former champion and title challenger.
Former long-reigning UFC champion and elite light heavyweight contender.
Repeat title-defense win over an elite rival; repeat and timing context keep it below max.
Ranked title challenger; strong defense with early-era depth context.
Dangerous elite UFC name, but not a clean champion-level or true top-five win in this timing context.
Experienced title challenger on a major unbeaten run; revenge and era context cap the credit.
Former UFC heavyweight champion and dangerous athlete, with timing and division context.
High-level future UFC champion, but earlier-career timing caps the credit.
Meaningful contender win before the title reign, below the later title-defense version.
Major elite name, but the UFC win came after his clearest non-UFC peak.
Solid UFC heavyweight win before Monson’s later contender run.

primeWindowDetail.endReason: Couture loss was recovered. Rampage II closes window; Jardine/Evans are decline context.

lossContextEvents.context: Liddell later beat Couture twice and built his championship reign.

shortCase: Chuck is the classic light heavyweight champion case: knockout aura, title defenses, Tito and Couture rivalries, and one of the defining star runs of the early modern UFC.
bestArgument: Chuck's case starts with title-reign value, star power, and rivalry wins over Couture and Ortiz during the UFC's breakout years.
counterArgument: Chuck’s argument is peak aura and era impact. The knock is that the resume is not as deep or clean as later light heavyweight and pound-for-pound cases.
whyRankedHere: Liddell ranks here because his light heavyweight title run and rivalry wins were central to the UFC’s breakout era. He had real championship volume, major star power, and a dangerous peak built around knockout threat.
whyNotHigher: He does not rank higher because later light heavyweight resumes are deeper and cleaner, and the late knockout losses drag the UFC-only profile hard.
finalTakeaway: Chuck is the classic UFC light heavyweight star: real title value and huge knockout aura, held back by a rough ending and deeper later-era resumes.
